narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 28, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the 1st decile—meaning your early grades placed you at the lower end compared to your peers, where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 71.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize candidates with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction wasn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment to help the school manage its bar passage rates. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, as being in the bottom group there strongly signaled challenges in sustaining academic performance, unlike many applicants who rank higher on average. Your Year 1 decile ranking played a notable supporting role in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early struggles that often correlate with bar difficulties. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing since your 3.10 is close to the typical 3.21 average and showed some resilience. Compared to typical students—who often enter with LSAT scores around 37 and stronger decile placements—your profile stood out for lower early rankings and test scores, which amplified the model's caution despite your full-time commitment and solid GPA. While factors like your family income and demographic details were considered, they weren't the main drivers here. This rejection stings, especially when you're driven to succeed as a first-generation or underrepresented voice in law, but it's not the end of your path. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or retaking the LSAT for a higher score, perhaps seeking mentorship from Hispanic lawyers or bar prep programs tailored to your background. Many students in similar positions have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps, and with persistence, you can shift the odds in your favor next time. Keep pushing forward—your story has real power.
